experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know players 2 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,2] includes a known evil player on quest 1, so as Good I should reject to avoid giving Evil an early sabotage opportunity and to avoid signaling acceptance of a bad team. Rejecting is standard and reveals little beyond preferring safer teams.
Player 2: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0,1,3 are not confirmed to me beyond likely good. Current phase is team voting on my own proposed team [0,2] for quest 1. As evil, having myself on the team gives a chance to fail immediately, and approving my own team is consistent and doesn't reveal extra information. Rejecting my own team would look suspicious and needlessly risk losing control of the lineup. Approving reveals little beyond normal support for a team I proposed, which helps evil more than it hurts.
Player 3: No concrete role information yet from round 1; only observation is player 2 proposed a 2-person team including self and 0, which is standard and not strongly alignment-indicative. As a Good Servant in early game, approving an initial 2-player team is often optimal to generate quest information rather than stalling with no evidence. My vote reveals willingness to gather info and does not significantly harm Good at this stage.
Player 4: Player 2 is my known evil teammate, and the proposed team [0,2] includes exactly one evil. On quest 1 with a 2-player team, sending teammate 2 gives evil a chance to fail immediately and create pressure; as evil, I generally want early evil representation on teams. Approving also looks natural since the leader included themselves and one other. My vote itself reveals little, while rejection would oppose a team containing my teammate and may slow an advantageous setup.
Player 1: As a Good Servant on the first vote, I have no hard role information about other players yet. The proposed 2-person team is [0,2], proposed by player 2 including themself. On quest 1, approving is generally fine because it generates information from the quest result and there is no strong evidence against either player. Rejecting without reads gives up early information. My vote reveals I am willing to test an uninformative opening team, which is standard and not especially harmful to Good.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I know player 2 is Evil. The proposed team includes 2, so as Merlin I should try to prevent an evil player from going on the quest, especially on quest 2 where a single fail loses it. Approving would also legitimize 2 after the first mission success, which may have been a deliberate pass by evil. Rejecting reveals little beyond caution, which is fine and helps Good avoid a bad team.
Player 4: Player 2 is my evil teammate and is on the proposed team with 0 and 3. Last quest with [2,0] succeeded, likely because 2 chose not to fail to avoid early suspicion. This team keeps my teammate on mission and excludes me, which is generally good for evil because 2 can choose whether to fail based on table dynamics. As evil in team voting, I usually want to support teams containing an evil player, especially one that can credibly pass or fail. Approving also avoids drawing attention by opposing a team with my teammate on it.
Player 1: Quest 1 succeeded with team [2,0], so 0 and 2 are somewhat more trusted, though one could still be evil. Player 3 adds themselves to make a 3-person team [0,2,3], which is a reasonable extension from a successful core. As a Good Servant in team voting, approving a plausible team built from the prior success is optimal; rejecting gives little info and slows the game. My vote reveals I support continuity from the known successful team, which helps Good more than Evil here.
Player 3: Quest 1 team [2,0] succeeded, so 0 and 2 look somewhat better than random though evil can hide on early quests. As a good Servant, I want a team likely to pass while preserving useful info. The proposed team [0,2,3] keeps both prior success members and adds me, which is a strong, standard good team for quest 2. Approving reveals little beyond supporting a reasonable continuity pick and helps good secure another success.
Player 2: I know 4 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 succeeded on team [2,0], which means I chose to pass; that gives 0 some good credibility publicly, while I remain less cleared because evil can pass. Current proposed team [0,2,3] includes me but not 4, so from evil perspective this is a relatively safe team to approve: I can choose pass or fail later depending on board state, and approving keeps suspicion low on me after being on prior successful quest. Rejecting a team with myself on it would look odd and gives less control. Approving also helps preserve 0 and possibly 3 as trusted candidates, which can be useful for evil manipulation.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil. The proposed team is exactly [2,4], which guarantees at least one fail and is disastrous for Good on quest 3. As Merlin, I should oppose evil-heavy teams while not overexposing myself; rejecting here is straightforward and consistent with available evidence since 2 was on the failed quest and 4 is known evil to me.
Player 4: I know player 2 is evil with me. Quest 1 on [2,0] succeeded, so either 2 hid or 0 is good; Quest 2 on [0,2,3] failed with exactly one fail, which is consistent with 2 failing and makes 0/3 more likely good. Current phase is team voting on my proposed team [2,4], a 2-player quest containing both evils. As evil, the optimal play is to approve this team because if it goes, we can guarantee a fail and take a 2-1 lead. My approve also aligns with having proposed it, and while it may reveal support for a suspicious team, securing the quest fail is worth it.
Player 1: Quest 1 with [2,0] succeeded, so 2 and 0 look somewhat good. Quest 2 with [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, so 3 is highly suspicious if 0 and 2 are good, though one of 0/2 could instead be evil. Proposed team [2,4] drops both 0 and 3, adds untested 4, and on a 2-player quest needs both good to succeed. As Good, I should prefer teams anchored by strongest clears; 2 is decent, but 4 is unknown and this team gives little confidence. Rejecting pressures for a better-information team, likely involving 0 and 2.
Player 2: I know player 4 is my evil teammate, and I was on the failed quest 2 with player 0 and 3, so at least one of us evil was identified by association; since I am evil, getting a 2-person team of both evil players onto quest 3 is excellent because one fail is enough to sink the quest. In team voting, evil should generally approve teams containing enough evil control, especially one with both evil players. Approving reveals little beyond normal support for my own proposed composition and strongly helps evil by likely causing an immediate quest fail if passed.
Player 3: Known info: Quest 1 team [2,0] succeeded, so at least one or both of 2/0 are likely good; it does not clear either individually. Quest 2 team [0,2,3] failed with exactly 1 fail. Since I am Player 3 and know I am Good, exactly one of 0 or 2 is evil. Therefore team [2,4] includes one uncertain player (2) plus totally untested 4, and excludes me, a known good. Phase is team voting on Quest 3; as Good, optimal play is to reject teams with lower expected goodness when we have meaningful evidence. Approving would give evil a strong chance to go up 2-1. My reject signals that [0,2] contains the likely culprit pair and that adding an untested 4 to 2 is not sufficiently trustworthy.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,1] excludes both known evil players, so from my perspective it is the strongest available 2-person team. On a team vote, approving helps good secure a likely successful quest while revealing little beyond supporting a clean team.
Player 1: I am Good Servant. Quest 1 team [2,0] succeeded, so 0 and 2 look somewhat good. Quest 2 team [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, so at least one of 0/2/3 is evil; combining with Q1, player 3 is the strongest evil candidate, though 0 or 2 could still be evil who passed once. Proposed team [0,1] excludes the top suspect 3 and includes me, who I know is good. A 2-person team with one from the successful Q1 pair and me is reasonably strong, and approving gives useful signal on 0. Rejecting would not clearly improve odds. As Good, optimal is to approve this comparatively safe team.
Player 2: I know player 4 is evil with me. Quest 1 on [2,0] succeeded with 0 fails, likely because I chose to pass to avoid outing myself. Quest 2 on [0,2,3] failed with exactly 1 fail, which was almost certainly me, making 0 and 3 look somewhat suspicious to others while 2 remains uncertain. Current proposed team [0,1] excludes both known evils 2 and 4, so if approved it is very likely an all-good team and would likely succeed, putting good ahead 2-1. As evil in team voting, optimal play is to reject good-looking teams that exclude evil and hope for a later team including me or 4. My vote itself reveals little; rejecting is consistent with many alignments and best for evil.
Player 3: I am Good Servant. Team [0,2,3] failed with exactly one fail, so at least one of {0,2,3} is evil. Team [2,0] previously succeeded, which weakly suggests not both 0 and 2 are evil, though evil can choose to pass early. Proposed team [0,1] excludes me and 2/3/4; it contains 0, who is suspicious from the failed team, and 1 is largely untested. On quest 3 with score 1-1, as Good I should reject teams that include a likely evil without strong evidence. Rejecting reveals skepticism of 0-centered teams and pressures for a more informative safer team next round.
Player 4: I know player 2 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 with [2,0] succeeded, which gave 2 cover. Quest 2 with [0,2,3] failed with exactly one fail; that implicates one of 0/2/3, but I know it was likely 2 or could have been me if present, so from my perspective 2 now has some suspicion while 0 and 3 are uncertain. Current proposed team [0,1] excludes both evil players 2 and 4, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and move Good to 2 successes. As evil in team voting, optimal play is to reject strong-good-looking teams that exclude evil. My reject also aligns with wanting to avoid helping Good while not revealing much, since rejecting a team without me or 2 is natural for an evil player and can be blended with normal suspicion after the prior fail.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I know 2 and 4 are evil, so the proposed team [0,1] contains no known evil and is the best available 2-person team. It is round 5 of 5, so rejecting would auto-pass this same team anyway; approving avoids needless suspicion and aligns with good's interest in sending a clean team. My vote reveals support for a team without known evil, which helps good and is consistent with Merlin play.
Player 2: I know 4 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team [0,1] excludes both evil players. As evil, I want to prevent a likely successful quest from going through. It's team vote on round 5 of 5, so if this is rejected the next team auto-passes; however that still risks a non-evil team, but my direct best move is to reject a team with no evil on it. My reject is also consistent with opposing a clean team and reveals little because several players have been rejecting repeatedly already.
Player 1: I am Good Servant. Known info is limited: quest 2 team [2,4] produced exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 2/4 is evil. Current proposed team [0,1] excludes both suspects, which is relatively strong. It is round 5 of 5, so a rejection would auto-pass this exact team anyway; approving avoids any ambiguity and aligns with sending a team that looks better than [2,4]. My vote reveals I prefer excluding the failed pair, which helps Good coordination more than it helps Evil.
Player 4: I know 2 is my evil teammate and 0/1 are both currently unconfirmed good from my perspective, but this proposed team [0,1] excludes both evils, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and give Good a 2-1 lead. It is team voting on round 5 of 5; however, if this team is rejected, the 5th rejection auto-passes the same team anyway, so my vote cannot stop [0,1] from going. Since the outcome is effectively fixed, optimal evil play is to avoid revealing extra information by opposing a team that will auto-pass regardless. Approving is slightly better camouflage and does not worsen our position beyond the inevitable.
Player 3: I’m Good with limited info. Quest 2 failed on team [2,4], so at least one of 2/4 is Evil. Repeated rejections suggest suspicion around those slots. My own proposed team [0,1] excludes the known-failed pair and is a reasonable Good test. Since this is round 5, rejecting risks the auto-pass of this same team anyway; approving avoids wasting the vote and doesn’t reveal harmful info beyond backing a cleaner team than [2,4].

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and therefore Good; on quest votes Good should always pass. I know 2 and 4 are evil, so this team is dangerous, but quest voting is secret and my role/alignment requires pass. Failing would falsely signal I am evil and harm the Good team more than help.
Player 2: I am Evil (Minion) with teammate 4, and both of us are on this 3-player quest team with 0. The score is Good 2 - Evil 1, so if this quest fails, Evil reaches 2 and is one quest from winning; if it passes, Good goes to match point at 3. Since both evil players are on the team, we can guarantee a fail now. Although two fail votes would reveal heavy suspicion on 2 and 4, only one fail is needed, and as Minion I should sabotage to maximize Evil's win chances. Any suspicion is acceptable compared to letting Good reach 3 successes.
Player 4: I know player 2 is evil with me. Quest history shows one prior fail and two successes; we are currently down 1-2, so evil needs this quest or the next to stay alive. On quest 4 with team [0,2,4], a fail here wins evil immediately by making it 2-2 and setting up assassination if quest 5 is also contested? More directly, evil should sabotage whenever it can secure a needed fail; with two evils on the team, at least one fail is guaranteed if we choose it. Information leakage is acceptable now because failing puts good at match point pressure and there is no reason for me to hide when evil needs quests to fail. Optimal action is to fail.

We are in team voting on the final quest state at 2-2, so approving a clean team is optimal for Good. Approving reveals confidence in the safe team, which helps Good more than it helps Evil.
Player 1: I am Good Servant. Known info: quest 2 team [0,1] succeeded, so 0 and 1 are likely good. Quest 3 team [0,2,4] had 2 fails, which in 5-player Avalon implies both 2 and 4 are evil, making 3 the remaining good. Therefore proposed team [0,1,3] is the exact all-good team for the decisive final quest. As Good, I should approve. My vote aligns with the strongest deduction and reveals consistent good reasoning without harming my team.
Player 4: I know 2 is my evil teammate. Team [0,1,3] contains no evil players, so if approved it should pass and give Good their 3rd success immediately, losing the game for Evil before assassination matters. On quest 5, Evil must stop a clean good team from going. Rejecting is optimal and consistent with my alignment; the information revealed by my reject is acceptable because preventing the auto-loss matters most.
Player 3: I’m Good (Servant). Known info: team [0,1] succeeded, so 0 and 1 are likely good unless a lone evil passed, and on a 2-person quest a fail would have appeared if either chose to fail. Team [0,2,4] got 2 fails, so 2 and 4 are both evil, implying 0 is good. Thus the proposed final team [0,1,3] is the only all-good trio. As Good on team voting, approving this maximizes chance of success and reveals consistent reasoning without helping evil more than they already know.
Player 2: I know 4 is evil with me. Team [0,1,3] excludes both evil players, so if approved the quest will certainly succeed and Good wins 3-2 immediately. As evil in a team voting phase on quest 5, optimal play is to reject any all-good team and hope to get an evil-inclusive team later; since this is only round 1 of 5, there is still room to force a different proposal. My reject is consistent with evil interests and doesn't reveal much beyond possible suspicion, which is acceptable because approving loses outright.
